topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, taped to the community board, felt like another piece of institutional detritus. Liam stared at the faded print, the words about the campus restructuring blurring slightly at the edges. He picked at a loose thread on his worn hoodie, the movement slow and deliberate. He didn't feel a sudden jolt of panic, just a dull thud in his chest, like a dropped weight settling in place. He glanced over at Maya, who was animatedly discussing weekend plans, her voice bright and full of energy. Liam offered a small, noncommittal hum in response, his gaze drifting toward the quad where students were already dispersing, their movements efficient and practiced. He shoved his hands into his pockets, the fabric rough against his palms, and started walking toward the library, not bothering to look back. The air felt heavy, unremarkable, like the usual late-afternoon humidity.

[story 2]

"So, that’s it, huh?" Sarah murmured, leaning against the brick wall of the administration building, tracing patterns in the dust with her shoe. She didn't look up when Mark finally spoke, his tone flat, devoid of any real inflection. "Yeah. Just... closing down. Whatever." Mark pulled his backpack tighter across his chest, the straps digging slightly into his shoulders. He watched the ivy crawling up the stone, noting the way the sunlight caught the green leaves—a predictable, constant spectacle. He took a slow sip of his lukewarm coffee, the bitterness coating his tongue without registering any real taste. The impending change didn't register as a crisis; it was just another administrative footnote in the sprawling narrative of his time here.

[story 3]

I found myself staring at the chipped paint on my desk, the pattern of the peeling veneer mesmerizing in its monotony. The email confirmation arrived, stark white on my screen, detailing the final liquidation date. I read it once, then twice, my eyes tracking the lines without absorbing any real meaning. There was no surge of anger, no wave of sorrow—just a quiet settling, a kind of profound stillness that settled over my limbs. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the metal legs echoing too loudly in the suddenly quiet room. I stood up, stretched my arms above my head, feeling the familiar, unhurried stretch in my muscles. It was just a change in scenery, a shift in the backdrop of my routine, and frankly, it didn't warrant any particular reaction.
